canonical_definition: Across the provided literature, token decoding is most commonly described as the process of generating a sequence of tokens one at a time during model inference. This behavior is operationalized and measured by its computational cost, with papers evaluating "average per-token decoding latency" and the "average FLOPs required to decode" a sequence ("Adaptive Rank Allocation: Speeding Up Modern Transformers with RaNA Adapters"). A prevalent strategy for this process is "Greedy decoding," which involves selecting the token with the highest probability at each step and is presented as a "maximum a-posteriori-based decoding" method. Some work contrasts the sequential, one-at-a-time approach with "Parallel decoding," an extension that generates multiple tokens per step by unmasking several positions at once. A less common framing, found in "The Foundations of Tokenization...", defines decoding more abstractly as the process of mapping a token sequence back into a character string, which may be stochastic. The concepts of "token-by-token" and "parallel" decoding are specifically discussed in the context of Masked Diffusion Models (MDMs).
expert_removed_reason: method/engineering — not a construct/measurement/behavior of LLM ability
papers: Adaptive Rank Allocation: Speeding Up Modern Transformers with RaNA Adapters
Better Instruction-Following Through Minimum Bayes Risk
Masked Diffusion Models are Secretly Time-Agnostic Masked Models and Exploit Inaccurate Categorical Sampling
Self-Improvement in Language Models: The Sharpening Mechanism
The Foundations of Tokenization: Statistical and Computational Concerns
The Hyperfitting Phenomenon: Sharpening and Stabilizing LLMs for Open-Ended Text Generation

definitions.text: Generating a sequence one token at a time by repeatedly selecting a masked position and filling it in.
definitions.paper: Masked Diffusion Models are Secretly Time-Agnostic Masked Models and Exploit Inaccurate Categorical Sampling

definitions.text: The fundamental process of generating a sequence of tokens, one at a time, during model inference, which can be measured for speed and computational cost.
definitions.paper: Adaptive Rank Allocation: Speeding Up Modern Transformers with RaNA Adapters

definitions.text: A decoding strategy where the model selects the token with the highest probability at each step of the generation process.
definitions.paper: Better Instruction-Following Through Minimum Bayes Risk

definitions.text: Generating multiple tokens per step by unmasking several positions at once rather than strictly one token at a time.
definitions.paper: Masked Diffusion Models are Secretly Time-Agnostic Masked Models and Exploit Inaccurate Categorical Sampling

definitions.text: Mapping a token sequence back into a character string, possibly stochastically.
definitions.paper: The Foundations of Tokenization: Statistical and Computational Concerns

This behavior is operationalized and measured by its computational cost, with papers evaluating "average per-token decoding latency" and the "average FLOPs required to decode" a sequence ("Adaptive Rank Allocation: Speeding Up Modern Transformers with RaNA Adapters"). A prevalent strategy for this process is "Greedy decoding," which involves selecting the token with the highest probability at each step and is presented as a "maximum a-posteriori-based decoding" method. Some work contrasts the sequential, one-at-a-time approach with "Parallel decoding," an extension that generates multiple tokens per step by unmasking several positions at once. A less common framing, found in "The Foundations of Tokenization...", defines decoding more abstractly as the process of mapping a token sequence back into a character string, which may be stochastic. The concepts of "token-by-token" and "parallel" decoding are specifically discussed in the context of Masked Diffusion Models (MDMs).
